coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for sensing Mg2+ ions in aqueous solution, and their application in the detection and imaging of intracellular Mg2+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Sadhanala, Hari Krishna
Aryal, Saurav
Sharma, Kusha
Orpaz, Ziv
Michaeli, Shulamit
Gedanken, Aharon
coredata.dc:description: The magnesium (Mg2+) ion is the second most abundant intracellular cation after potassium, and it is involved in a variety of biological processes and physiological functions. Because of the different effects which are dependent on Mg2+ ion concentration, it is critical to monitor Mg2+ ion levels in biological systems. Here, we report the hydrothermal synthesis of photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) using 4-Hydroxybenzaldehyde and 1, 2, 4, 5-benzenetetramine tetrahydrochloride as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The as-synthesized NCDs demonstrated excitation dependent photoluminescence (PL) with a quantum yield of 16.2%. Because of water dispersibility and chelating functional groups, NCDs were used for highly selective detection of Mg2+ ions using ratiometric PL enhancement with a detection limit of 60 μM. Following that, based on highly biocompatibility and sensing of Mg2+ ions in aqueous solutions, NCDs were employed as photoluminescent probe to detect the Mg2+ ions of mammalian cell lines such as J774, HeLa, and Hek293T, which is most likely due to effective complex formation between NCDs and the intracellular Mg2+ ions. As far as we could possibly know, this is the first report of aqueous solution dispersed carbon dots for intracellular sensing and imaging of Mg2+ ions based solely on an increase in NCDs PL intensity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnesium ions
Carbon-Dots, Nitrogen doping
LOD

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Imaging of Mg2+ in J774 cell lines

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Chemistry, Bar Ilan University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors would like to acknowledge Dr. Ilana Perelshtein from Department of Chemistry, Bar Ilan University for HR-TEM analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) TEM and HRTEM images of NCDs.
(a) Full XPS survey spectra of NCDs, (b-e) High resolution XPS C1s, N 1 s and O 1 s spectra.
(a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) UV-Visible and PL excitation spectra of NCDs. Inset of Fig. 3b is the optical image of NCDs under UV light illumination (365 nm).

(a) Excitation-dependent PL of NCDs, (b) Selectivity of NCDs in the presence of different metal ions (500 µM), (c) PL spectra of NCDs at various concentrations of Mg2+ ions. (d) PL intensity ratio as function of Mg2+ ion concentration.
(a-c) PL response of NCDs towards J774, Hela, and HeK293T cell lines. (d) Optical image of NCDs with Mg2+ ions, and cell lines.
(a and b) Dark field images of J774 cell lines with RNCDs and NCDs.
Graphical illustration for the synthesis of N-doped carbon dots (NCDs).
Sensing illustration of NCDs for Mg2+ ions in the aqueous media.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for sensing Mg2+ ions in aqueous solution, and their application in the detection and imaging of intracellular Mg2+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and the BINA center, Bar-Ilan-University, Ramat-Gan 52900, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the BINA center, Bar-Ilan-University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and the BINA center, Bar-Ilan-University, Ramat-Gan 52900, Israel

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Mina and Everard Goodman Faculty of Life Sciences and Advanced Materials and Nanotechnology Institute, Bar-Ilan University, Ramat-Gan 52900, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Mina and Everard Goodman Faculty of Life Sciences and Advanced Materials and Nanotechnology Institute, Bar-Ilan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Mina and Everard Goodman Faculty of Life Sciences and Advanced Materials and Nanotechnology Institute, Bar-Ilan University, Ramat-Gan 52900 Israel.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The magnesium (Mg2+) ion is the second most abundant intracellular cation after potassium, and it is involved in a variety of biological processes and physiological functions. Because of the different effects which are dependent on Mg2+ ion concentration, it is critical to monitor Mg2+ ion levels in biological systems. Here, we report the hydrothermal synthesis of photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) using 4-Hydroxybenzaldehyde and 1, 2, 4, 5-benzenetetramine tetrahydrochloride as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The as-synthesized NCDs demonstrated excitation dependent photoluminescence (PL) with a quantum yield of 16.2%. Because of water dispersibility and chelating functional groups, NCDs were used for highly selective detection of Mg2+ ions using ratiometric PL enhancement with a detection limit of 60 μM. Following that, based on highly biocompatibility and sensing of Mg2+ ions in aqueous solutions, NCDs were employed as photoluminescent probe to detect the Mg2+ ions of mammalian cell lines such as J774, HeLa, and Hek293T, which is most likely due to effective complex formation between NCDs and the intracellular Mg2+ ions. As far as we could possibly know, this is the first report of aqueous solution dispersed carbon dots for intracellular sensing and imaging of Mg2+ ions based solely on an increase in NCDs PL intensity.

ce:section.ce:para: The magnesium ion (Mg2+) has been known for over a century as an essential element for a wide assortment of physiological functions and cellular processes [1,2]. After potassium, it is one of the most abundant intracellular divalent cations. Astonishingly, Mg2+ ions regulate over 350 enzymatic activities in the body [3]. Furthermore, Mg2+ ions are directly engaged in a variety of biological activities, including protein and nucleic acid production, energy metabolism, signal transmission, cytoskeleton maintenance, and ion transport pump control [4–6]. An imbalance of Mg2+ ions cause many diseases such as impaired renal function and failure, muscle spasms and convulsions, diabetes mellitus, bronchial asthma, neuromuscular problems, cardiovascular disease, and hypertension [7–11]. As a result, the research of magnesium ion homeostasis, which investigates the biological roles of magnesium ions via magnesium-selective ion channels, has gotten a lot of attention. The use of analytical methods to detect and quantify Mg2+ ions in living cells in real time has yet to be substantially explored.

It is acknowledge that the whole cellular concentration of Mg2+ ions has been calculable to be 14–20 mM, of that a lot of is present in mitochondria as bound metal and also the free Mg2+ ion within the cytoplasm is estimated to be 0.3–1.5 mM [12,13]. Initially, a number of research groups centered on determining the concentration of the free Mg2+ ion by exploiting Mg2+-sensitive dyes inside the living cells. Therefore, quantifying the concentration of intracellular Mg2+ ions could be a major challenge and needs the development of bio-friendly materials capable of selectively quantifying the intracellular Mg2+ ion concentration using easy analytical techniques. For example, flame atomic absorption qualitative analysis (F-AAS) has been used to substantiate intracellular Mg2+ ion concentration in cells or tissues. However, this methodology needs millions of cells or milligrams of tissue. Therefore, the employment of F-AAS is neither economical nor sensible for biological applications. Alternative analytical methods such as graphite chamber atomic absorption spectrometry and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) are used to quantify Mg2+ ions in living cells. Though they need solely a little quantity of sample, the analysis requires advanced and pricey instrumentation and extremely complicated analytical protocols that don't seem to be offered in most standard laboratories. Moreover, all these analytical techniques are unable to distinguish between free and bound Mg2+ metal ions inside cells and to determine the overall intracellular magnesium distribution. Therefore, the development of sensors for the quantification and visualization of Mg2+ ions within the cytoplasm and nuclei of living cells is of great demand [14,15].

Because of its ease of use, quick response, and low cost, the fluorescence approach, the most adaptable technology, has been employed for the detection and quantification of metal ions in environmental samples. For the detection of Mg2+ ions, fluorescent compounds like calix[4]arene, crown ether, salicylaldehyde Schiff base, carboxylic acid, and polyether are utilized [16–22]. However, due to their insolubility in water, these aren't totally suitable for intracellular detection of Mg2+ ions. Extremely fluorescent "KMG-104-AsH" dyes have been reported to be employed for highly selective Mg2+ ion concentration measurement in mitochondria [23,24]. However, these dyes have only been used to detect Mg2+ ions in cells, not to quantify them. Recently, Iotti et al. described using a fluorescent dye supported by the organic compound DCHQ5 to detect and quantify Mg2+ ions intracellularly. A difficult synthesis technique, long preparation, and a long purifying process are all disadvantages of this technology. As a result, developing a fluorescent probe with a simple synthesis approach for intracellular detection and quantification of Mg2+ ions remain a difficult issue.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been employed for metal ion detection, bio-sensing, and bio-imaging for the past two decades due to their chemical stability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and economic feasibility [25–32]. Generally, there are few reports on turn-on CDs, but they have gotten a lot of interest because of their practical uses as compared to their turn-off counterparts [33]. Cu2+ ions have been detected by turn-on fluorescence of CDs utilizing aggregation-induced enhanced emission (AIEE), while Zn2+ ions have been detected using chelation-enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) of CDs [34–36]. Recently, our group reported that boron-doped CDs can detect Mg2+ ions in non-aqueous media but not in aqueous media [37]. In response to the aforementioned issues, we developed novel fluorescent nitrogen doped CDs for detecting Mg2+ ions in aqueous media, which are also used for intracellular detection and imaging of Mg2+ ions.

In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal technique from 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde and 1,2,4,5-benzenetetramine tetrahydrochloride and examined for the detection of magnesium ions in aqueous solutions using intense turn-on fluorescence at 365 nm with a limit of detection (LOD) of 60 µM. The NCDs were subsequently studied for intracellular detection and imaging of Mg2+ ions based on the ratio metric turn-on fluorescence results.

4-Hydroxybenzaldehyde, 1, 2, 4, 5-Benzenetetramine tetrahydrochloride, Quinine sulphate, and salts like Mg(CH3COO)3·4H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, HgCl2, FeCl3·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, CuCl2, Zn(CH3CO2)2·2H2O, Ni(CH3COO)2·4H2O, CoCl2, SrCl2·6H2O, Ca(CH3COO)2·xH2O, Mn(CH3COO)3·2H2O, and NaCl were procured from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals. Dae Jung Chemicals Korea supplied the sulfuric acid. Without further processing, all the analytical compounds obtained were utilized. All studies were conducted using ultrapure water (18.2 M).

NCDs were made by dissolving 2 mmol of 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde and 1 mmol of 1, 2, 4, 5-benzenetetramine tetrahydrochloride in 20 ml of water and stirring the resulting solution for 10 min to homogenize it. The entire solution was then transferred to a 30 ml stainless steel autoclave and heated for 6 h in an oven at 180 °C. The oven was cooled to room temperature after the reaction was finished. After centrifuging for 30 min at 8,000 rpm, the dark yellow solution was filtered through a 0.22-micron syringe filter. Finally, the clear and transparent yellow-colored filtrate was dialyzed for 24 h with a 500–1000 Da dialysis bag, and the resulting solution was stored in the refrigerator for further use.

The nanostructures of NCDs were studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a JEOL JEM −2100 with a high angle annular dark field (HAADF) detector (accelerating voltage of 200 kV, Gatan USC 4000 4×4K camera). The functional groups were determined using the Thermo Scientific NICOLET iS10 Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (FT-IR) and Raman measurement was performed by using Jobin Yvon Micro Raman (λ = 532 nm). Thermo Scientific Nexa spectrometer with UHV (2.5 ×10–10 Torr base pressure) and Al K anode source at 15 kV was used to determine the chemical composition of the materials. A Cary Eclipse Varian spectrophotometer and a spectrofluorophotometer were used to evaluate the fluorescence and UV-Vis spectra of the NCDs at room temperature using a Xe lamp as an excitation source.

1 ml of the obtained NCDs solution was diluted with 10 ml of 1% aqueous PBS solution, and 10 mM of various metal ion solutions was prepared using ultrapure water in a typical approach. To detect the NCDs, 2.5 ml of diluted NCDs were titrated individually with 10 mM metal ion solutions, and immediate fluorescence measurements were taken at a wavelength of 400 nm. Similarly, 10 mM Mg2+ ion solutions were titrated with dilute NCDs solutions for sensitivity investigation, and subsequently fluorescence spectra were acquired at room temperature using a Varian spectrofluorophotometer. For all the experiments, we used the same procedures.

J774, HeLa, and Hek293T were chosen as the three mammalian cell lines for this study. Hela and Hek293T cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 (sigma) supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated FBS, whereas J774 macrophages were cultivated in DMEM (sigma) supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated FBS. The cells were grown to confluence, collected, and washed three to four times in a 10% PBS solution. For future use, they were disseminated in a 5% PBS solution. The cells were thoroughly shaken to ensure uniform dispersion before the experiment. Various amounts of cell lines were introduced to 2.5 ml of NCDs probe solution for intracellular detection of magnesium ions, and fluorescence measurements were taken right away. The fluorescence ratio (I/I0) of NCDs in the presence of the cell lines was computed, and the Mg2+ ion concentration in the J774 cell lines was determined solely using the NCDs calibration curve.

J774 cells cultured as described above were incubated with various concentrations of NCDs or without NCDs (control) and then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay (Sigma) was performed according to the manufacturer's instructions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of Mg2+ in J774 cell lines
ce:section.ce:para: The grown J774 cells were treated with10 μl NCDs (1 mg/ml) solution for 24 h. Then, the cells were viewed under the microscope after incubation, and fluorescence was recorded at their individual emission spectra using a confocal microscope. Scheme. 1.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were used to analyze the microstructure and size of N-doped carbon dots (NCDs). The narrow size distributions of NCDs with homogeneous quasi-spherical shape and well distributed are shown in Fig. 1a. The absence of resolved lattice fringes in the HRTEM image of the NCDs, as seen in Fig. 1b, indicates that the NCDs are amorphous in nature [38]. The average size of NCDs was calculated be ~ 5 nm (inset of Fig. 1b). Scheme. 2.

The structural configuration and surface composition of NCDs were studied using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS spectra shows three distinct peaks associated with carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen atoms at 285, 400, and 530 eV ( Fig. 2a), with elemental contents of 67.41%, 13.68%, and 18.91%, respectively. The C 1 s spectra was decomposed into two peaks about 285 and 287 eV, corresponding to the CC/C–C and C–O/C–N bands as shown in Fig. 2b [39]. Two peaks about 399 and 403 eV were assigned to pyrrolic and graphitic N, respectively, in the fitted high-resolution N 1 s spectra (Fig. 2c) [40]. The O 1 s spectra was split into two peaks at 531 and 535 eV, which were assigned to CO and C–O bonds (Fig. 2d) [41]. As a result, the XPS data clearly demonstrated that N doping in CDs was successful. The NCDs were subjected to Fourier transfer infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) to extract information about the functional groups, and the results from FT-IR corroborated the XPS study. Fig. 3a reveals clear peaks at 700.6, 1055.1, 1247.4, 1391.6, 1614.0, 1711.6, 2973.5, 3338.5, and 3428.6 cm-1, which correspond to =C-H bending, C–OH, C–N, O–H bending, CC, CO, =C–H stretching, N–H, and O–H vibrational modes [42–44]. The XPS and FT-IR analyses revealed the presence of oxygen and nitrogen surface groups in the NCDs as well as N doping in the CDs. Raman analysis of NCDs was performed to determine the structural properties of NCDs, and Fig. S1 shows two bands at 1384 and 1588 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands, with an ID/IG ratio of 0.5, implying that as-prepared NCDs are structurally less defective.

In order to explore the optical properties of the synthesized NCDs, ultraviolet-visible light and photoluminescence (PL) measurements were performed. As shown in Fig. 3b, the strong absorption peak at 215 nm is designated as the π-π⃰ electronic transition in the sp2 domain. Two peaks at 275 and 350 nm and a small shoulder peak at 380 nm correspond to n-π⃰ electronic transition of the oxygen and nitrogen functional groups [45]. NCDs showed two PL excitation wavelengths at 318 and 400 nm, respectively. Fig. 4a shows that NCDs exhibits excitation-dependent PL behavior. When the excitation wavelength is changed from 300 nm to 450 nm, the emission wavelength shifts to the red region, which may be due to different surface conditions or dimensional changes [46]. NCDs showed blue emission under the best dilution of ultraviolet radiation. Using quinine sulfate as a reference compound, the quantum yield (QY) of NCDs was calculated to be 16.2%.

Before the Mg2+ ion was detected, the stability of NCDs was investigated at various pH levels. It was noticed that the PL intensity of NCDs increased from pH-2.0–6.0 and then gradually decreased as the pH increased, possibly due to deprotonation,25 indicating that NCDs PL intensity is pH dependent (Fig. S2a). A high ionic strength NaCl environment was used to study the stability of NCDs further, and NCDs showed no PL intensity change at all NaCl concentrations (Fig. S2b). As a result, the PL intensity of NCDs is pH dependent, whereas it is nearly independent in NaCl solution. Using a spectrofluorometer, the NCDs explored for the detection of Mg2+ ions based on the inherent PL property. A specific concentration of different metal ions (10 mM) was added to optimally diluted NCDs, and the PL was monitored at 365 nm excitation to evaluate the selectivity, ratio of peak intensities at 481 and 408 nm (I481/ I408), of the NCDs towards metal ions (Figs. S3a and 4b). The NCDs were found to have high selectivity for Mg2+ ions as compared to other metal ions (inset of Fig. 4b), with no interference from Ca2+ ions, the most prevalent interferer, and only minor interference from Cu2+, Cd2+, and Mn2+ ions. Furthermore, the influence of most common ions such as Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions on Mg2+ ion detection using NCDs is investigated. It is noticed that Fe2+ has no effect, whereas Fe3+ has a negligible influence on Mg2+ ion detection (Fig. S3b), which could be due to electron transfer from NCDs to Fe3+ in the excited state. This is because of strong affinity of carbonyl and hydroxyl groups on NCDs towards Mg2+ ions and this interaction further enhanced by the nitrogen incorporation, which increases the electron density over NCDs through delocalization, thereby improves the sensitivity for Mg2+ ions [47]. Fig. 4c shows that when the amount of Mg2+ ions added to the NCDS rises, the intensity of the peak PL at 408 nm drops, whereas the peak at 481 nm grows at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The peak intensity ratio (I481/I408) plotted against Mg2+ ion concentration to determine the detection limit (LOD) for NCDs for Mg2+ ions (Fig. 4d). NCDs have a LOD of 60 µM, which calculated from the lower concentration range using the mathematical formula 3σ/K, where K is the slope of the fitted curve and σ is the standard deviation of the intercept.

The recovery of the NCDs sensor was investigated using EDTA titration, as shown in Fig. S4a. It was observed that after adding certain amount of 1 mM of EDTA solution to NCDs-Mg2+ complex solution, 90% of NCDs sensor was recovered with reduced PL intensity, which is probably due to interference from the formed EDTA-Mg2+ complex. In order to disclose the sensing mechanism for aggregation induced enhanced emission (AIEE) of NCDs by Mg2+ ions, UV-Visible spectral analysis of NCDs in the presence of different concentrations of Mg2+ ions was accomplished. As seen in Fig. S4b, as the Mg2+ ion concentrations increase, the peak intensity at 334 nm begins to decline more rapidly than peak at 289 nm, while a new peak about 390 nm emerges. The appearance of new peak at 390 nm clearly indicating that n-π * transitions (CO, O–H, N–H) are involved in Mg2+ion sensing and aggregation of NCDs in the presence of Mg2+ ions [37], with the emergence of a new peak likely due to the formation of NCDs- Mg2+complexes [48]. Furthermore, to further support the AIEE process, a dynamic light scattering study for NCDs was performed without and with Mg2+ ions (Fig. S5). The hydrodynamic diameter of NCDs increased from 24.3 to 459 nm after Mg2+ ions were added, and results were in agreement with literature reports [37].

Because aqueous NCDs efficiently recognize Mg2+ ions, investigations on intracellular recognition and imaging of Mg2+ ions have been undertaken utilizing these NCDs without surface labeling. For intracellular detection of magnesium ions at room temperature, different million (M) number of each mammalian cell line, including J774, HeLa, and Hek293T, was added to the PBS buffered NCDs probe solution. In the presence of J774 cell lines, Fig. 5a demonstrates the varied PL intensity of NCDs at 408 and 480 nm, which is similar to Mg2+ ion titration with NCDs (Fig. 4c). Furthermore, other mammalian cell lines, such as the HeLa and Hek293T cell lines, displayed similar NCDs PL intensity variations to the J774 cells (Fig. 5b and c). As a result, these findings show that NCDs may detect Mg2+ ions in living cells. The J774 cell lines are more sensitive to NCDs than the HeLa and Hek293T cell lines, as demonstrated in Fig. 5d. In addition, a calibration curve illustrated in Fig. 4d was used to determine the quantity of Mg2+ ions in these cell lines. Mg2+ ion concentrations in 1 million J774, HeLa, and Hek293T cells were determined to be 0.40, 0.18, and 0.15 mM, respectively. The quantified results are in good concordance with other analytical techniques stated results.12,13.

The NCDs were subsequently studied based on the foregoing observations in order to locate Mg2+ ions in the cell lines. The cytotoxicity of the NCDs at various doses was evaluated with J774 cells using the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay prior to the bioimaging experiment to determine their suitability for use in cell imaging. Our findings reveal that NCDs are not hazardous to cells and so can be used to image J774 cells (Fig. S6). Magnesium content in macrophages is particularly important biologically since magnesium is involved in the immunoregulatory system. Magnesium-containing implants used for cartilage regeneration have better clinical outcomes because of its regulatory role (PMID: 29467509). For bioimaging, mammalian J774 cell lines were stained with 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) (Fig. S7) and reported N-doped carbon dots (RNCDs) derived from BSA, used as a control [49], and as synthesized NCDs for 24 h before being analyzed under a confocal microscope at 405 nm excitation. The dark-field images of J774 cells in Fig. 6a and b clearly show that very little emission was observed for the cells containing RNCDs, whereas cells containing NCDs have a strong blue color, which is due to the formation of strong complexes between pre-existing Mg2+ ions of J774 cells and oxygen functional groups of NCDs. Thus, our NCDs are useful for identifying Mg2+ ions in mammalian cell lines, which has been verified. Therefore, we can conclude from the preceding findings that our aqueous NCDs can be employed to detect and visualize Mg2+ ions in cells effectively.

In conclusion, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were prepared using 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde and 1,2,4,5-benzenetetramine tetrahydrochloride as carbon and nitrogen sources by a simple hydrothermal method. The as-prepared NCDs showed blue color under UV illumination with quantum yield of 16.2%. The NCDs buffered with 1% PBS showed excellent selectivity towards Mg2+ ions with ratiometric fluorescence turned on, while interfering Ca2+ ions turned off the fluorescence of NCDs at 365 nm excitation. The LOD of NCDs for Mg2+ was calculated to be 60 µM. As a result of these findings, NCDs were used for intracellular Mg2+ ion detection and imaging. Even further, from the calibration curves of NCDs sensor, quantification of Mg2+ ions within cell lines was performed. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of aqueous carbon dots for intracellular Mg2+ ions detection with high selectivity.

Dr. Hari Krishna Sadhanala has conducted the research under the supervision of Prof. A. Gedanken. Mr. Saurav Aryal and Ziv Orpaz have helped in the biological cells experiments and they were supervised by Prof. S. Michaeli. Dr. Kusha Sharma has helped in recording the confocal measurements.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to acknowledge Dr. Ilana Perelshtein from Department of Chemistry, Bar Ilan University for HR-TEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131958.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary materialElectronic Supplementary Information (ESI) available: [Raman analysis (Fig. S1), pH and NaCl effect on PL of NCDs (Fig. S2), Selectivity of NCDs for different metal ions and interference of Fe2 + and Fe3 + ions in the Mg2 + ion detection (Fig. S3). Recovery and UV-Visible study (Fig. S4), DLS study (Fig. S5), cytotoxicity study (Fig. S6), and confocal images of J774 cell lines (Fig. S7), and LOD comparison table (Table S1)]
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 8-Hydroxyquinoline derivatives as fluorescent sensors for magnesium in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.M. Alexandra, A ribosomal strategy for magnesium deficiency, Sci. Signaling, 9 (2016) 269.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reducing ribosome biosynthesis promotes translation during Low Mg2+ stress

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.H. Pontes, J.Yeom, E.A. Groisman, Reducing Ribosome Biosynthesis Promotes Translation during Low Mg2+ Stress. Mol. Cell, 64 (2016) 480–492.

P.W. Flatman, Mechanisms of magnesium transport, Annu. Rev. Physiol. 53 (1991) 259–271. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev.ph.53.030191.001355.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective fluorescence sensing of Mg2+ions by Schiff base chemosensor: Effect of diamine structural rigidity and solvent

B. 4 (2016) 2913–2920. https://doi.org/10.1039/c6tb00519e.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Hari Krishna Sadhanala got his Ph.D. from Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore, India under the supervision of Prof. K. K. Nanda. He is currently a postdoctoral Fellow at bar-Ilan University under the guidance of Prof. Aharon Gedanken, Israel.

Prof. (Em.) Aharon Gedanken obtained his Ph. D. degree from Tel Aviv University, Israel. After his postdoctoral research at USC in Los Angeles, he got a lecturer position at BIU on Oct. 1975. He spent two sabbatical years at AT&T Bell Laboratories in 1980–8 l, and 1987–88. He also has done research at NIDDK, NIH in the summers of 1989, 1990 and 1991. In 1994 he switched his research interest from Spectroscopy to Nanotechnology. His special synthetic methods of nanomaterials include: Sonochemistry, Microwave Superheating, Sonoelectrochemistry, and Reactions under Autogenic Pressure at Elevated Temperatures (RAPET). Since 2004 he is mostly focused on the applications of nanomaterials. Gedanken has published 870 per-reviewed manuscripts in international journals, and has applied for 38 Patents. His H-Index is 97 according to the WEB of SCIENCE. Gedanken has served as the Department Chairman as well as the Dean of the Faculty of Exact Sciences at Bar-Ilan University. He is on the editorial boards of 6 international journals. He still leads a group of 10 research people. He was a partner in 14 EC projects in FP5, FP6, FP7 and Horizon 2020. He was the Israeli representative to the NMP (Nano, Materials, and Processes) committee of the EC in FP7. He was awarded the prize of the Israel Vacuum Society in 2009 and the Israel Chemical Society for excellence in Research in Feb. 2013. Two of his products made it to the market and are sold successfully.
